text: <@UG0EL18H3> I do something in Mu that I haven't seen very often (but that seems similar to Go within a single package): I allow types to be used before declaration, which makes it easier to reorganize code. The compiler manages holes in its table of type definitions. But it still reports holes as errors after reading all of the input source. Maybe there's further to take it here.

attachments.title: Profiler & Audio Visualization
attachments.video_html: <iframe width="400" height="225" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/hgxt0YlWULI?feature=oembed&autoplay=1&iv_load_policy=3" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>

elements.text: Slightly too big for
- here's an update on things I've done recently in the Audio part of the project.

text:
Towards the end of the video I show a profile of the sample music.  For comparison, here's a single frame of the UI on a release build.  The UI is locked at 60fps; so can't go faster than that, but you can also see a couple of audio frames on this screenshot (which don't necessarily align inside frames), along with the FFT thread at the bottom.
Here 512 frames of audio is being generated in about 1.2ms - this is the equivalent of about 11ms of audio.  Although I keep an eye on this stuff, and build things sensibly to avoid bottlenecks, I haven't actually spent too much time optimising anything yet.  But it's good to know there is headroom.
I also forgot to mention in the video that one of the main things the profiler brings to the table is debugging; it already showed me a place where I'm doing unexpected work, and a mutex lock that should not be happening.   For the small effort it took to build this feature, I think it was well worth it.

Although I keep an eye on this stuff, and build things sensibly to avoid bottlenecks, I haven't actually spent too much time optimising anything yet.  But it's good to know there is headroom.
I also forgot to mention in the video that one of the main things the profiler brings to the table is debugging; it already showed me a place where I'm doing unexpected work, and a mutex lock that should not be happening. text:
Hey folks, I'm Aria :wave:

Like most people here, I daydream FOC ideas :smile: I'm usually thinking about the gaps that separate groups of people, and how tools and languages can bridge those gaps.

I've been _trying_ to do this as my job since 2014, but only managed to get there three years ago. I've been funding it with consulting, grants, and two very helpful angel investors.

What I'm working on is an authoring environment where developers and designers make things together. I usually describe it as a design tool that "blurs the line between designer/developer, author/consumer, and artist/scientist." If that sounds lofty, it's because it is. Right now it's just a modest animation editor for web apps that can be used by both designers and developers.

Short-term, it scratches an old itch. I always wanted to draw and code the same kind of stuff.

Long-term, I think this kind of environment (one that bridges manual and procedural creation), can be a building block to other FOC applications, like data/interactive journalism, exploratory learning, and trans-disciplinary authoring environments.

Also as a side-project, I'm building a personal memex. (_who isn't?_ :man-shrugging:)

Anyway, I'm always up for a <https://twitter.com/ariaminaei|call> to bounce ideas :blush:

text:
This thread’s discussion (on Apple + privacy) was interesting to me: <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=25078034> Nobody really suggested the alternative being end-user + true personal computing. The analogy I use is that what we call ‘personal computing’ is really restaurant made food delivered to our homes very quickly (vs true personal cooking where I can make the food I want at home using tools that are personal-human-scale).

The common answers to centralized computing / centralized internet like handwaving ‘decentralized’ or ‘p2p’ or ‘open source / libre’ I strongly feel are WORSE options for most people (minus the philosophy). That’s like taking a restaurant kitchen & food-assembly-line and putting it on every street corner for the neighborhood to run (for most people this is worse than a regular restaurant). _(the analogy isn’t perfect I know!)_ Personal, home cooking is fundamentally different than restaurant made food in qualitative ways that make it appealing in its own right and not let people get caught up in their ‘resistance’ of centralized restaurants.

The other great insight from food industry is that centralized food creation and personalized food creation can co-exist just fine, and people can move between both systems as they please.

text: This is a false dichotomy - I would argue that p2p systems and libre software is a _precondition_ to "person-human-scale" tools and computing. Without p2p you are beholden to network structures, and without libre software you can't modify your system in the first place. The remaining problem is one of UX, which has largely been ignored as you point out, and is something that should be worked on. But I think making a dichotomy like that is throwing the baby out with the bathwater. "Person-human-scale" tools are useless without the structural factors to make them useful—resurrecting hypercard, for  example, is not going to solve the problem of every program you run being monitored by apple or whatever.

But I think making a dichotomy like that is throwing the baby out with the bathwater. "Person-human-scale" tools are useless without the structural factors to make them useful—resurrecting hypercard, for  example, is not going to solve the problem of every program you run being monitored by apple or whatever.

text: Oh for sure, I’m not disagreeing with the importance of developing the underlying protocols like p2p. But often the discussion ends with JUST the technology, like…ALL we need is the tech. I think the design principles should lead the tech, not the other way around (obv in reality these things are a bit more entangled), especially since the end user conveniences of modern computing (URL’s are dope!) is what lead companies to innovate our way to our current situation. Thinking only about the tech means that personal computing will remain in the hands of open source hackers who want to compile their own kernels.

text: I love this analogy. I think it nicely sidesteps a thought-virus I have when thinking about approaches to these things: “That won’t scale”. Home cooking doesn’t scale up into restaurant cooking, and that’s okay. They’re different things with different qualities.

But our society seems only capable of making such large scale investments in projects that exacerbate a user/developer divide. Open source/free software is not going to cut it

text: I empathize with the need for better, focused funding here, bring back the good ol PARC days, etc. I think good funding can be helpful in fighting the good-enough-ness that current computing offers. The funding can help make sure that academia, ACM, CS journals, computer companies, hardware vendors, etc don’t only cater to the existing system. It can also help design hardware that’s better suited for this type of end user computing (think one-laptop-per-child but less politicized!).

elements.text: I empathize with the need for better, focused funding here, bring back the good ol PARC days, etc. I think good funding can be helpful in fighting the good-enough-ness that current computing offers. It can also help design hardware that’s better suited for this type of end user computing (think one-laptop-per-child but less politicized!).

text:
One counter point though is that other end-user-heavy markets / use cases didn’t necessarily have this. Cooking, knitting, house cleaning, etc all evolved naturally and ‘have refused to die’ (in the good sense). What can we learn from those?

• These tend to be rooted in family / cultural traditions that are passed down, sideways, etc. Bret talks about ‘horizon of observability’ as a key property of shared cognitive tools (e.g. war-room maps)
• Many of these were old traditions & crafts that have been around a while
• These tend to REALLY celebrate unique tastes. There’s not 1 way to make Chicken Biriyani, there’s literally millions of ways. Then you throw in fusion cuisine, etc.
• The tools tend to have admirable & desirable properties for learning. Easy to get started, hard to master. Spatial / tactile / tangible, no compiling kernels or arbitrary abstract BS (humans have evolved amazing physics priors). 
• I’m sure there are others, I suppose Bret / Dynamicland had a similar line of thinking and are aligning their work around that
I think ultimately these other end-user-heavy endeavors have been quite anti-fragile and resistant to centralization, over-corporatization, etc. To be clear, I don’t think centralization is ‘bad’ but it is counter to the ideals & goals of end-user computing.

text: I think an interesting distinction is the timescale. For knitting for example (due to some recent googling), hand knitting had been around for at least 500 years by the time the first knitting machine was made. It was much more well-established compared to computing, which has gone from not existing in any form, to our current industry-dominated model in about O(50) years.

text:
I think another issue here is that ‘personal computing’ is such a HUGE area. It’s not a single activity or set of activities like cooking, it’s like ’anything that’s Turing complete and emulates some existing set of activities + has new activities native to the medium [like video games]’.

What’s challenging is that even new end-user focused tools, like Para for dynamic drawing (<http://jenniferjacobs.mat.ucsb.edu/>), have to be designed for the current computing environment.

attachments.title: Jennifer Jacobs
attachments.text: Assistant Professor at University of California Santa Barbara in Media Arts and Technology. Director of the Expressive Computation Lab which investigates ways to support expressive computer-aided design, art, and manufacturing by developing new computational tools, abstractions, and systems that integrate emerging forms of computational creation and digital fabrication with traditional materials, manual control, and non-linear design practices.

text:
> I would also argue that we have more of the tech already ready, but the UX is nowhere close IMO.

Perhaps predictably, I'd push back on this. The tech we have ready is for running restaurants. We have to recreate it for the equivalent of home cooking. From a standing start. In the face of entrenched competition. Without a huge centralized funding source. Thinking of it as purely a UX problem is doomed, I think. Existing tech assumes reliable funding.

text:
Yes, I think so.  Though I can imagine other uses for it.  You could possibly imagine a simplified version which shows the live coder which parts of their performance are taking the most time.  That's probably just looking for a hammer now I've got the nails though ;)
Also, I think I will add frame candles so you can see spikes in performance and indications of memory allocations.  I'll put these things in as I need them.
I looked for an ImGui profiler before doing this one; I found  one that didn't handle threads very well, or profile events that spanned frames.  I found another that was a butchered version of the first one, with better UI but worse profiling.  In the end, this only took a few hours over the course of a week to do; I'm pleased I put in the effort.  My version will be OS in a week or so when I've finished tweaking it and pushed it to my shared library.
I'm not claiming this is fast, but for my purposes it is 'fast enough' :slightly_smiling_face: It allocates all the memory up front, and records simple time stamps, so overhead is fairly decent, which is all I need.

text: Kinda funny: <https://aiweirdness.com/post/634684907050909696/what-to-do-with-your-time>

attachments.title: What to do with your time
attachments.text: In Leonardo DaVinci’s diary you can find his to-do lists, the kinds of broad-ranging investigations the famous polymath would use to occupy his time: Draw Milan. Get the master of arithmetic to show you how to square a triangle Ask Giannino the Bombardier about how the tower of Ferrara is walled Why is the fish in the water swifter than the bird in the air when it ought to be the contrary since the water is heavier and thicker than the air? Describe the tongue of the woodpecker. But since DaVinci has already done all that, and we’re all not busy enough (pause to stare at camera) clearly we need new things to do. Blog reader Emily Davis suggested getting a neural net to generate more to-do items for the modern DaVinci. I prompted GPT-3 with 9 items from DaVinci’s to-do lists, and here are some of the highlights: GPT-3 did seem to know that it was generating DaVinci content - often it would finish the list of to-do items with a pronouncement on DaVinci’s legacy, and many of its suggestions were plausible: “Get the friar minor to tell me what is the best time to water a plant or tree.” “Get Maestro Leonardo Fioravanti of Bologna to tell me the measurements of the world and the distances between the stars.” To get GPT-3 to add giraffes to the list, I had to set things up so that GPT-3 thought it was completing a blog post in which a giraffe-obsessed neural net had generated DaVinci to-do items. It’s all very meta, but it worked! I collected the rest of them in a bonus post, which you can get by signing up here. My book on AI, You Look Like a Thing and I Love You: How Artificial Intelligence Works and Why it’s Making the World a Weirder Place, is available wherever books are sold: Amazon - Barnes & Noble - Indiebound - Tattered Cover - Powell’s - Boulder Bookstore
attachments.fallback: AI Weirdness: What to do with your time

text: And yes, it reminds the turtle from Logo. But here you make the program tangible and everything is in sight. And I guess it is one step further in the vision of Seymour Papert. With Logo he wanted to offer microworlds to children so they can feel mathematics: by programming with Logo they "play computer in their mind" (or not the computer, the turtle) to imagine the appropriate "math" instructions. Here we don't need to imagine, but we manipulate the concept directly with instant reaction. Really cool!

text:
<@U01EHAE9D0W> hah, this is actually not terribly far off from what I'm planning to build next year
```(defn Lagrangian-action
  "Generates a scalar [[Action]] for the supplied path `q`
   in a system governed by the [[Lagrangian]] `L`."
  [L q t1 t2]
  ([[Calculus/definite-integral]]
     (compose L (Gamma q)) t1 t2))```
But with a layman audience b/c of it's structure hopefully.

text:
It's rather long, but the high points are
• Online textbooks are bad paper on screens, why not take advantage of the new hardware underpinnings
• Roam is *fantastic,* and everyone should use it. Why are textbooks linear anyway? 
• Examples are the way to go, and parameterized examples are better. Live examples are best. 
• (e.g. physics book where every single concept was encoded in a math language *in the book*, and the examples had clear dependencies to the formulas defined *in the book*)
• What happens when each function in the book has its own page with examples, definitions, code, worked math problems, etc...
• Allow users to put additional material in the book to expound on their thoughts 
• Crowd source better material over time - books don't need to be static. Look at wikipedia.
• Everyone learns different, so why force all to use the same book? Why not have multiple versions of each page tailored to different learning types?

text: I would rather not have memes or other low-effort content, except in the rare case there's an actual discussion to be had about it. text:
We've had this exact discussion a few times now, and the <#C01932BJGE8|present-company> channel was created as a catch-all for this sort of content. It's for stuff that's not quite "on-topic" for the future of computing, but not quite off-topic either. The name "Present Company" is a play on words:
• If you want to talk about matters of present-day computing, this is the channel
• If you want to enjoy the company of others via lighthearted or playful digressions, this is the channel
We're using this name instead of "Random" or "Off-topic" because we don't want to encourage _only_ that sort of stuff there. It's more open than that.

No, I'm not going to create a new channel just for memes and jokes at this time. Please post all memes and jokes in <#C01932BJGE8|present-company>. I don't for a second believe that the channel will devolve into frustrating noise and pabulum — on balance, this community has good taste. If the discussion there becomes too banal, I'll make adjustments. Not gonna do that defensively, though, because I'd rather let a thousand flowers bloom, you know?

text:
You can use an <img> tag that points to a URL, and that works fine. Put any styling on the <img> inline for best compatibility, rather than using classes or ids and a separate style block. `px` units work best in my experience.

Eg — here's an image I use in one of my emails:

```<img width="113px" height="126px" style="vertical-align:middle; margin-left:6px; margin-top: 20px;" src="[Big long CDN url]">```
